stimulus: We have a moral obligation not to destroy books, even if they belong to us. The reason is quite simple: If preserved, books will almost certainly contribute to the intellectual and emotional enrichment of future generations.
questionStem: Which one of the following most accurately expresses the principle underlying the argument?

options.A: It is morally incumbent upon us to devote effort to performing actions that have at least some chance of improving other people’s lives.
options.B: We are morally obligated to preserve anything that past generations had preserved for our intellectual and emotional enrichment.
options.C: The moral commitments we have to future generations supersede the moral commitments we have to the present generation.
options.D: We are morally obligated not to destroy anything that will most likely enrich, either intellectually or emotionally, for posterity.
options.E: Being morally obligated not to destroy something requires that we be reasonably assured that that thing will lead to the betterment of someone we know.

stimulus: The southern half of a certain region of the earth was covered entirely by water during the Cretaceous period, the last 75 million years of the Mesozoic era, the era when dinosaurs roamed the earth. Dinosaurs lived only on land. Thus, plesiosaurs—swimming reptiles that lived during the Cretaceous period exclusively—were not dinosaurs. No single species of dinosaur lived throughout the entire Mesozoic era.
questionStem: If the statements in the passage are true, each of the following could be true EXCEPT:

options.A: Dinosaurs inhabited the northern half of the region throughout the entire Mesozoic era.
options.B: Plesiosaurs did not inhabit the southern half of the region during the Cretaceous period.
options.C: Plesiosaurs did not inhabit the southern half of the region before the Cretaceous period.
options.D: Dinosaurs did not inhabit the northern half of the region during the Cretaceous period.
options.E: Dinosaurs inhabited the southern half of the region throughout the entire Mesozoic era.

stimulus: Essayist: Wisdom and intelligence are desirable qualities. However, being intelligent does not imply that one is wise, nor does being wise imply that one is intelligent. In my own experience, the people I meet have one or the other of these qualities but not both.
questionStem: If the essayist’s statements are true, then each of the following could be true EXCEPT:

options.A: Most people are neither intelligent or wise.
options.B: Most people are both intelligent and wise.
options.C: No one is both wise and intelligent.
options.D: No one is either wise or intelligent.
options.E: Many people are intelligent and yet lack wisdom.

stimulus: Concerned citizen: The mayor, an outspoken critic of the proposed restoration of city hall, is right when he notes that the building is outdated, but that the restoration would be expensive at a time when the budget is already tight. We cannot afford such a luxury item in this time of financial restraint, he says. However, I respectfully disagree. The building provides the last remaining link to the days of the city’s founding, and preserving a sense of municipal history is crucial to maintaining respect for our city government and its authority.
questionStem: So to the question, "Can we really afford to?" I can only respond, "Can we afford not to?" Which one of the following most accurately characterizes a flaw in the concerned citizen’s argument?

options.A: The argument is solely an emotional appeal to history.
options.B: The argument ambiguously uses the word "afford."
options.C: The argument inappropriately appeals to the authority of the mayor.
options.D: The argument incorrectly presumes that the restoration would be expensive.
options.E: The argument inappropriately relies on the emotional connotations of words such as "outdated" and "luxury."

stimulus: Obviously, we cannot in any real sense mistreat plants. Plants do not have nervous systems, and having a nervous system is necessary to experience pain.
questionStem: The conclusion above follows logically if which one of the following is assumed?

options.A: Any organism that can experience pain can be mistreated.
options.B: Only organisms that have nervous systems can experience pain.
options.C: Any organism that has nervous system can experience pain.
options.D: Only organisms that can experience pain can be mistreated.
options.E: Any organism that has nervous system can be mistreated.

stimulus: Inez: In these poor economic times, people want to be sure they are getting good value for their money. I predict people would be more willing to buy antiques at our fair if we first have the object inspected by professional appraisers who would remove any objects of questionable authenticity. Anika: I disagree with your prediction. Our customers already are antiques experts. Furthermore, hiring professional appraisers would push up our costs considerably, thus forcing us to raise the prices on all our antiques.
questionStem: Anika’s response proceeds by

options.A: indicating that a particular plan would have an effect contrary to the anticipated effect
options.B: claiming that a particular plan should not be adopted because, while effective, it would have at least one undesirable consequence
options.C: arguing that an alternative plan could achieve a desired result more easily than the plan originally proposed
options.D: questioning the assumption that authorities are available who have special knowledge of the problem under discussion
options.E: offering a counterexample in order to show that a particular general claim is too broadly stated

stimulus: In some ill-considered popularizations of interesting current research, it is argued that higher apes have the capacity for language but have never put it to use—a remarkable biological miracle, given the enormous selectional advantage of even minimal linguistic skills. It is rather like claiming that some animal has wings adequate for flight but has never thought to fly.
questionStem: Which one of the following is most similar in its reasoning to the argument above?

options.A: Arguing that there are some humans who never sleep is rather like discovering a species of lion that does not eat meat.
options.B: Arguing that Earth has been visited by aliens from outer space is rather like claiming that early explorers had visited North America but never founded cities.
options.C: Arguing that the human brain has telekinetic powers that no humans have ever exercised is rather like arguing that some insect has legs but never uses them to walk.
options.D: Claiming that some people raised tobacco but did not smoke it is rather like claiming that a society that knew how to brew alcohol never drank it.
options.E: Arguing that not all people with cars will drive them is rather like claiming that humans invented gasoline long before they used it as fuel for transportation. they used it as fuel for transportation.

stimulus: Sarah: Some schools seek to foster a habit of volunteering in their students by requiring them to perform community service. But since a person who has been forced to do something has not really volunteered and since the habit of volunteering cannot be said to have been fostered in a person who has not yet volunteered for anything, there is no way this policy can succeed by itself. Paul: I disagree. Some students forced to perform community service have enjoyed it so much that they subsequently actually volunteer to do something similar. In such cases, the policy can clearly be said to have fostered a habit of volunteering.
questionStem: Paul responds to Sarah’s argument using which one of the following argumentative techniques?

options.A: He argues that Sarah is assuming just what she sets out to prove.
options.B: He argues that Sarah’s conception of what it means to volunteer excludes certain activities that ought to be considered instances of volunteering.
options.C: He introduces considerations that call into question one of Sarah’s assumptions.
options.D: He questions Sarah’s motives for advancing an argument against the school policy.
options.E: He argues that a policy Sarah fails to consider could accomplish the same aim as the policy that Sarah considers.

stimulus: Sarah: Some schools seek to foster a habit of volunteering in their students by requiring them to perform community service. But since a person who has been forced to do something has not really volunteered and since the habit of volunteering cannot be said to have been fostered in a person who has not yet volunteered for anything, there is no way this policy can succeed by itself. Paul: I disagree. Some students forced to perform community service have enjoyed it so much that they subsequently actually volunteer to do something similar. In such cases, the policy can clearly be said to have fostered a habit of volunteering.
questionStem: The main point at issue between Sarah and Paul is whether

options.A: there are any circumstances under which an individual forced to perform a task can correctly be said to have genuinely volunteered to perform that task
options.B: being forced to perform community service can provide enjoyment to the individual who is forced to perform such service
options.C: being forced to perform community service can by itself encourage a genuine habit of volunteering in those students who are forced to perform such service
options.D: it is possible for schools to develop policies that foster the habit of volunteering in their students
options.E: students who develop a habit of volunteering while in school are inclined to perform community service later in their lives

stimulus: Only computer scientists understand the architecture of personal computers, and only those who understand the architecture of personal computers appreciate the advances in technology made in the last decade. It follows that only those who appreciate these advances are computer scientists.
questionStem: Which one of the following most accurately describes a flaw in the reasoning in the argument?

options.A: The argument contains no stated or implied relationship between computer scientists and those who appreciate the advances in technology in the last decade.
options.B: The argument ignores the fact that some computer scientists may not appreciate the advances in technology made in the last decade.
options.C: The argument ignores the fact that computer scientists may appreciate other things besides the advances in technology made in the last decade.
options.D: The premises of the argument are stated in such a way that they exclude the possibility of drawing any logical conclusion.
options.E: The premises of the argument presuppose that everyone understands the architecture of personal computers.

stimulus: Sociologist: Research shows, contrary to popular opinion, that, all other things being equal, most people who have pets are less happy than most people who do not. Therefore, any person who wants to be as happy as possible would do well to consider not having a pet.
questionStem: Which one of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the sociologist’s argument?

options.A: Some people who have pets are happier than most people who do not.
options.B: Most people who have no pets occasionally wish that they had pets.
options.C: Most people who have pets are reasonably happy.
options.D: Most people who have pets feel happier because they have pets.
options.E: All people who have no pets admit to feeling unhappy sometimes.

stimulus: The dwarf masked owl, a rare migratory bird of prey, normally makes its winter home on the Baja peninsula, where it nests in the spiny cactus. In fact, there are no other suitable nesting sites for the dwarf masked owl on the Baja peninsula. But a blight last spring destroyed all of the spiny cacti on the Baja peninsula. So unless steps are taken to reestablish the spiny cactus population, the dwarf masked owl will not make its home on the Baja peninsula this winter.
questionStem: The argument depends on assuming which one of the following?

options.A: No birds of prey other than the dwarf masked owl nest in the spiny cactus.
options.B: If the Baja peninsula contains spiny cacti, then the dwarf masked owl makes its winter home there.
options.C: On occasion the dwarf masked owl has been known to make its winter home far from its normal migratory route.
options.D: The dwarf masked owl will not make its winter home on the Baja peninsula only if that region contains no spiny cacti.
options.E: Suitable nesting sites must be present where the dwarf masked owl makes its winter home.

stimulus: At night, a flock of crows will generally perch close together in a small place—often a piece of wooded land—called a roost. Each morning, the crows leave the roost and fan out in small groups to hunt and scavenge the surrounding area. For most flocks, the crows’ hunting extends as far as 100 to 130 kilometers (60 to 80 miles) from the roost. Normally, a flock will continue to occupy the same roost for several consecutive years, and when it abandons a roost site for a new one, the new roost is usually less than eight kilometers (five miles) away:
questionStem: Of the following claims, which one can most justifiably be rejected on the basis of the statements above?

options.A: Crows will abandon their roost site only in response to increases in the population of the flock.
options.B: When there is a shortage of food in the area in which a flock of crows normally hunts and scavenges, some members of the flock will begin to hunt and scavenge outside that area.
options.C: Most of the hunting and scavenging that crows do occurs more than eight kilometers (five miles) from their roost.
options.D: Once a flock of crows has settled on a new roost site, it is extremely difficult to force it to abandon that site for another.
options.E: When a flock of crows moves to a new roost site, it generally does so because the area in which it has hunted and scavenged has been depleted of food sources.
